skill.name: refactor-assistant
skill.description: Guides safe code refactoring with analysis, suggested patterns, incremental edits, and test-focused review.
skill.summary: Find refactoring opportunities and apply safer structural code improvements.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static findings for external command execution, weak cryptography, and network reconnaissance are false positives from Markdown examples and normal refactoring text. No prompt injection, exfiltration, or malicious intent was found. The remaining risk is low because the skill can guide file edits during refactoring.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Refactoring Capability
low_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to make incremental code changes and run tests after refactoring. This is expected for a refactoring assistant, but users should review edits before accepting them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly describes making changes and running tests. This is legitimate behavior for the skill, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported command execution locations are Markdown usage examples and language examples, not Ruby shell backtick execution. No executable shell logic or command injection path is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are fenced Markdown examples. They do not contain shell execution primitives or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Finding Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography location is the skill description text. No cryptographic algorithm, hash function, encryption routine, or security-sensitive implementation appears there.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is metadata describing automated refactoring. There is no evidence of cryptographic code or weak algorithm usage.

low_findings.title: Static Network Reconnaissance Finding Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: The reported network reconnaissance location is a refactoring pattern name about renaming code symbols. No scanning, probing, network access, or external endpoint is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 25 only mentions renaming variables, functions, or classes. I found no evidence of network reconnaissance behavior.

content.value_statement: Large or aging codebases accumulate duplication, complex functions, and unclear structure. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code analyze code, suggest refactoring patterns, and apply careful incremental changes.
actual_capabilities: Identifies code smells such as long functions, duplicate logic, and large classes.
Suggests refactoring patterns including extract function, extract class, and simplify conditionals.
Ranks refactoring opportunities by impact and risk.
Guides incremental code changes after user approval.
Encourages test execution and backward compatibility checks.
limitations: It depends on the available project context and test coverage.
It cannot guarantee behavior preservation without reliable tests.
Major architecture changes still need human design review.
It may need language-specific guidance for uncommon frameworks.

use_cases.title: Reduce Technical Debt
use_cases.description: Find duplicated logic, oversized functions, and confusing names before a planned cleanup sprint.

use_cases.title: Prepare Safer Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Break a risky refactor into smaller reviewed steps with clear impact notes.

use_cases.title: Improve Legacy Code
use_cases.description: Analyze older modules and identify changes that improve readability without changing behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Find Simple Refactors
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this file for simple refactoring opportunities. Focus on duplication, naming, and long functions. Do not edit yet.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritize Cleanup Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this module and group refactoring opportunities by high, medium, and low priority. Include risk and expected benefit for each item.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Incremental Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor the selected function using small behavior-preserving steps. Run or identify relevant tests after each meaningful change.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Architecture Boundary
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this package for responsibility boundaries. Propose an incremental refactor plan that preserves public APIs and minimizes migration risk.

output_examples.input: Review a service class with repeated validation and a long save method.
output: Identifies duplicated validation as a medium-priority issue.
Suggests extracting validation into a shared helper.
Lists behavior risks and tests to run before editing.

output_examples.input: Refactor a complex conditional in a payment workflow.
output: Explains why the conditional is hard to maintain.
Recommends a clearer strategy or dispatch structure.
Applies changes in small steps when approved.

output_examples.input: Plan cleanup for a legacy module before a release.
output: Separates safe readability changes from risky design changes.
Prioritizes security and correctness issues first.
Documents follow-up work for a later release.

best_practices: Run relevant tests before and after each refactoring step.
Ask for a suggestion-only pass before approving edits to critical code.
Keep refactoring changes separate from feature changes when possible.
anti_patterns: Using the skill to rewrite large systems in one unreviewed change.
Accepting structural edits when no tests or manual checks exist.
Mixing formatting churn with behavior changes in the same request.

faq.question: Does this skill write code changes?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide implementation after approval, with an emphasis on small behavior-preserving edits.

faq.question: Can it run tests?
faq.answer: The skill instructs the assistant to run or identify relevant tests when the environment supports that workflow.

faq.question: Is it limited to one programming language?
faq.answer: No. The guidance is language-neutral and uses common refactoring patterns across many codebases.

faq.question: Can it guarantee no behavior changes?
faq.answer: No. Tests and human review are still needed to confirm behavior preservation.

faq.question: Is it useful for code review?
faq.answer: Yes. It can identify maintainability risks and propose safer cleanup steps before a pull request.

faq.question: Does it include security scanning?
faq.answer: It can notice security concerns during refactoring, but it is not a dedicated security scanner.
